[6/14/26](#6/14/26)

[LOG](#log)

# Introduction {#introduction}

This document is a collection point for information about the LC29H GNSS RTK-GPS receivers. It mainly focuses on setting up a base station.

There are two categories of RTK-GPS receivers:

1. L1/L2 band receivers such as the UBlox ZED-F9P, which cost around $250 and up  
2. L1/L5 band receivers such as the LC29H and a number of others which tend to cost much less.

The L5 band may not be fully implemented yet \- all 24 satellites are supposed to be up by 2027 \- the current status and any consequent issues are not known.

# LC29H Hardware Overview {#lc29h-hardware-overview}

The LC29H is a L1/L5 module from Quectel that’s based on the Airoha AG3335 chip, which is a 12nm advanced GNSS receiver chip designed for high-precision, low-power positioning. Airoha provides some base firmware, and Quectel layers their firmware on top. The LC29H family comes in two interesting (to us) flavors:

* LC29H (BS) for base station use  
* LC29H (DA) for rover use

Waveshare makes RPi hats with these modules on them \- available from Amazon for $73 (including supplied antenna) for each unit.  
LC29H (DA): [Amazon.com: Waveshare LC29H Series Dual-Band GPS Module for Raspberry Pi, Dual-Band L1+L5 Positioning Technology, Supports RTK Rover to Realize High-Precision Centimeter-Level Positioning](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0CGX69XJP?ref=ppx_yo2ov_dt_b_fed_asin_title)   
LC29H (BS):  [Amazon.com: Waveshare LC29H Series Dual-Band GPS Module for Raspberry Pi, Dual-Band L1+L5 Positioning Technology, Supports RTK Base, Can Establish a Base Station to Transmit Correction Data](https://www.amazon.com/Waveshare-Dual-Band-Positioning-Technology-Correction/dp/B0CGX8V2JF) 

See the Amazon listing for lots of details, dimensions etc.

You also need to buy an ML1220 battery for the LC29H units. The ML1220 is a rechargeable lithium battery used to hold up NVM.  
[https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0DSZVD6CL?ref=ppx\_yo2ov\_dt\_b\_fed\_asin\_title](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0DSZVD6CL?ref=ppx_yo2ov_dt_b_fed_asin_title) 

The Waveshare device can be used either as an RPi HAT, or as a micro-USB serial port device. Configure the jumpers to select which mode and route signals appropriately: Position A is micro-USB serial, position B is HAT. Position C passes RPi serial through to LC29H micro-USB \- it’s use is unknown.

The Waveshare boards mount as hats on an RPi zero, 3, 4 etc. An antenna is provided with them, though it’s not as big (good?) as the Ublox antenna. There are other vendors who sell stand-alone boards with a USB-serial interface to the Quectel ship and SMA antenna connectors. They can be as inexpensive as $20-30 \- but they may not be the RTK versions \- caveat emptor\! It’s hard to see exactly which version is on offer, and only the (DA) and (BS) versions will give you RTK fixes.

## Waveshare wiki {#waveshare-wiki}

The wiki has lots of info on what SW to use and how to configure it with the Quectel QGNSS app. Beware: some of it is wrong. [LC29H(XX) GPS/RTK HAT \- Waveshare Wiki](https://www.waveshare.com/wiki/LC29H\(XX\)_GPS/RTK_HAT?srsltid=AfmBOop0tXy2Ik9J1sucHWu41gsjH3b4QV8nq0sEc3OUC3l_CGaAoF6L) 

## LC29H versions {#lc29h-versions}

The DA version is used on a rover. The Quectel QGNSS windows app supports the LC29HDA as well as an LC29HBA and many other LC29 and other LC chips. However, the LC29H(BA) is not the same as the LC29H(BS). You can’t select the LC29H(BS) in the app, and base station setup doesn’t work properly. See the Waveshare wiki which has a completely different protocol spec for the LC29H(BS) than the BA/DA/Other versions. Examples of other versions:

* LC29H (BA) supports DR and RTK.   
* LC29H (CA) only supports DR.  
* LC29H (DA) only supports RTK.

# LC29H Software Overview {#lc29h-software-overview}

## ROS integration {#ros-integration}

The Quectel LC29H (DA) GNSS/RTK rover module is integrated into ROS 2 primarily through serial-based NMEA parsers or specialized GitHub repositories such as [https://github.com/phryniszak/lc29h](https://github.com/phryniszak/lc29h)  or [https://github.com/amaltp13/GPS-RTK-Localisation](https://github.com/amaltp13/GPS-RTK-Localisation) . Alternatively, generic NMEA drivers (e.g., [https://github.com/geodnet/geopulse\_ros](https://github.com/geodnet/geopulse_ros) ) can parse the NMEA strings published over the serial port (typically 115200 bps). The base station is not integrated into ROS2, and does not need to be.

## RTK Corrections {#rtk-corrections}

RTK works by supplying corrections from a fixed base station (which could be a LC29H(BS) or an internet-resident L1/L5 corrections source) to rovers (LC29H (DA)). Based on the corrections, rovers can calculate position relative to the base station to an accuracy of a couple of centimeters. The LC29H chips use the L1 and L5 GNSS bands.

Very few rtk2go sites offer L1/L5 corrections, so you almost have to have your own base station (which can be shared by multiple rovers in the same general area). The base station can be an rtk2go reference station.

# Using the QGNSS App to connect to the base station {#using-the-qgnss-app-to-connect-to-the-base-station}

Open the [Waveshare Wiki](https://www.waveshare.com/wiki/LC29H\(XX\)_GPS/RTK_HAT?srsltid=AfmBOooN5FIkS2fNU3xfwcKuo2eZ5A7IUS9NhGsLtwSIC0H8m00jqYhd#Software) in a Windows PC and go to the Software section at the bottom of the page, where there are links to zip files. Download the zip files for QGNSS and CP210x and unzip them. Install the CP210x driver if not already installed on Windows.

Launch QGNSS (the Quectel LC29H app) and proceed as follows to connect QGNSS to the base station:

* Connect the LC29H (BS) to the Windows PC  
* Use the gear icon in QGNSS to select Model: LC29HBA  
* Select the COM port the device was assigned to (e.g. COM9). Leave baud rate at 115200  
* Click OK. It should connect, and display connected status at the bottom. The top left button is the connect/disconnect control. You should see Total Times incrementing

Sky view is deceptive \- don’t rely on it. Use the View Menu to access GNSS Signal View, Text Data, Binary Data and Messages View.

Open Signal View and look for L5 (GPS), E5A (Galileo) and B2a (BeiDou) on Signal Level to verify the high precision L5 satellites

Open Messages view. You should see RTCM3 message 1005 incrementing. This is the most coarse corrections data.

Open Text view to monitor what’s sent & received from the device.

Open Tools \-\> Command console. This window lets you type in multiple commands you might want to send, add a checksum to them (Checksum button), send them to the device,, and save the commands for later recall. 

# LC29H Messages {#lc29h-messages}

The variants of LC29H support some standard NMEA commands (like $GNGGA and $GNVTC etc, but use a lot of proprietary messages as well. There are two variants of proprietary NMEA commands. In NMEA, proprietary commands begin with $P followed by 3 characters that are a manufacturer code.

* $PAIRxxx \- Proprietary commands developed by Airoha  
* $PQTMxxx \- Proprietary commands developed by Quectel

You will see them displayed in the text data console, and will use some of them. Some of them are documented in the two Quectel LC29H GNSS Protocol Specifications, and some are not.

See the section [Useful base station commands](#useful-base-station-commands) for messages that can be sent as commands to the LC29H 

# Set up base station \+ corrections source (Windows) {#set-up-base-station-+-corrections-source-(windows)}

You have to configure a corrections source that can provide corrections to rovers. There are three main components to a corrections source:

1. Base station. This must be surveyed in, meaning it’s position must be precisely established. RTK position reports on the rover will be relative to the position the base station is configured at. You can either tell the base station its position (apriori knowledge, such as an external survey marker), or you can let it run and average its readings over an extended period of time, and assume that the average error is zero over enough samples.You also need to set parameters to get it to output high-res corrections.  
2. SNIP Caster: This software takes a corrections stream and makes it available to many clients locally or on the internet.  
3. NTRIP server: This software reads correction data from the base station hardware and pushes the stream into something that can use it. This may be rover SW directly, or a caster.

You may configure the rover to query a caster for corrections.

## Survey In the base station (Approach \#1, works) {#survey-in-the-base-station-(approach-#1,-works)}

You really have to survey-in the base station with a Windows machine, because the Quectel app filters output so you can view only text (e.g. NMEA sentences) and suppress the slew of binary data that is in the stream.

**Note:** Within the Windows section of the Waveshare Wiki the RTK Base section gives instructions for setting up the LC29H(BS). However it is missing some important steps, including $PQTMSAVEPAR then reset, and enabling MSM7 messages. In case of problems, RTFM\! But be warned \- the Quectel\_LC29H(BS)\_GNSS\_Protocol\_Specification\_V1.0.pdf is helpful but incomplete \- e.g. it doesn’t define the $PQTMSVINSTATUS message. **Don't follow the steps in the Waveshare Wiki\!**

Setting up the survey-in requires configuring the observation criteria, monitoring the accuracy convergence, grabbing the final coordinates, and saving the configuration.

Download the Quectel QGNSS zipfile from the Waveshare wiki and unzip it. Launch QGNSS.exe.

### Step 1: Start the Survey-In {#step-1:-start-the-survey-in}

Open the **Command Console** (Tools \-\> Command Console) in QGNSS. You will issue a command that defines how long to survey and what target 3D accuracy to achieve.

Send the following command format:

Plaintext  
$PQTMCFGSVIN,W,1,\<Duration\>,\<Accuracy\>,0,0,0

### **Parameters:** {#parameters:}

* **W**: Write mode.  
* **1**: Enables Survey-In mode.  
* **\<Duration\>**: Minimum observation time in seconds. For an enduring base station, **86400** (24 hours) or at least **3600** (1 hour) is recommended. Note: the spec calls this seconds, but it’s actually number of observations (emitted every second) which meet the validity requirement. It could take longer.  
* **\<Accuracy\>**: Target 3D position accuracy limit in **decimeters** (e.g., 15 means $1.5 meters).

**Example (3600 seconds, 1.5m accuracy threshold):**

Plaintext  
$PQTMCFGSVIN,W,1,3600,15,0,0,0

*Note: Type the command into QGNSS and make sure to click the **"CheckNum"** or **"Checksum"** button in the console to append the correct \*XX tail before hitting **Send**.*

### Step 2: Monitor Accuracy and Progress {#step-2:-monitor-accuracy-and-progress}

To track the progress of the survey-in and watch the accuracy converge, enable the live status message:

$PQTMCFGMSGRATE,W,PQTMSVINSTATUS,1,1  
Once sent, you will see periodic $PQTMSVINSTATUS streaming in your NMEA log window:

$PQTMSVINSTATUS,\<MsgVer\>,\<TOW\>,\<Fix\_type\>,\<Res0\>,\<Res1\>,\<Obs\>,\<CfgDur\>,\<MeanX\>,\<MeanY\>,\<MeanZ\>,\<MeanAcc\>\*XX

### **How to Check if it's Finished:** {#how-to-check-if-it's-finished:}

1. **Fix\_type is 0: no fix, 1: 3D fix, 2: Differential / RTK fix. My experience is it changes from 1 to 2 when the survey completes.**  
2. **\<Res1\> is the survey state indicator. 02 means survey in progress, 12 means survey complete.**  
3. **\<Obs\> vs \<CfgDur\>**: The \<Obs\> value (current observed seconds) will count up. When \<Obs\> matches or exceeds \<CfgDur\>, the time requirement is satisfied. If \<Obs\> doesn’t increment, most likely your requested accuracy is less than the accuracy of incoming fixes, and no observations are being accepted for averaging.  
4. **\<MeanX\>,\<MeanY\>,\<MeanZ\> \- the updated mean ECEF position values**  
5. **\<MeanAcc\>**: This field displays the current mean position accuracy achieved **in decimeters**.

### Step 3: Extract and Fix the Base Coordinates {#step-3:-extract-and-fix-the-base-coordinates}

Once the status indicates the survey is complete (\<Fix\_type\> is 11 or 12), capture the **\<MeanX\>, \<MeanY\>, and \<MeanZ\>** ECEF coordinate values directly from that streaming status message.

* **Stop the status messages** (to clear up the log):

$PQTMCFGMSGRATE,W,PQTMSVINSTATUS,0,1

To transition the module from an active "surveying" state into a stable, permanent "Fixed Base" state using those exact coordinates, send the absolute positioning write command:

$PQTMCFGSVIN,W,2,0,0.0,\<MeanX\>,\<MeanY\>,\<MeanZ\>  
e.g. Paul’s command was $PQTMCFGSVIN,W,2,0,0.0,-645592.0271,-5305914.5807,3468700.3156

*(The 2 sets it to Fixed ECEF Mode, bypassing any future survey-in execution on reboot).*

### Step 4: Save Configuration & Open RTCM Output {#step-4:-save-configuration-&-open-rtcm-output}

To make sure your settings survive a power cycle, save the parameters to the flash memory and configure your RTCM3 streams.

* **Save to Flash**:

$PQTMSAVEPAR

* **Read back what you saved, and check it**

$PQTMCFGSVIN,R

* **Turn on RTCM Outputs**: Ensure your base station is actively outputting the correction packets (like the 1005 antenna position and satellite observations):

$PAIR432,1  \# Enables MSM7 correction data, which is bigger but enables faster better fixes  
$PAIR434,1   \# Enables RTCM3.x Type 1005 (Base Position)  
$PAIR436,1   \# Enables RTCM3.x Ephemeris/Observation streams

* **View binary data** Ensure you are getting RTCM3 MSM7 messages like 1127, 1097, 1087  
* **Press the reboot button and view RTCM outputs**. You will likely find the MSM7 messages are replaced by MSM4 messages like 1074, 1124\. This means the MSM7-enable is not being saved in the configuration, and your software should (ideally) re-enable MSM7 after a reboot.  
* **Re-read the ECEF data.** Make sure it hasn’t been lost over the reboot

$PQTMCFGSVIN,R\*26

## SNIP Setup (Windows) {#snip-setup-(windows)}

SNIP (Simple NtrIP server) is a windows caster app. It is used by [rtk2go.com](http://rtk2go.com). You can run it locally for test purposes or to provide corrections to your rover.

* At [use-snip.com](http://use-snip.com) create an account and purchase ($0) an eval version. This can be converted to a lite (3 streams max) version by going to Help \-\> Registration. The product starts up with 4 Relay Streams \- remove them. The eval version stops after an hour. It can be restarted, but that’s annoying \- I converted it to a lite version which doesn’t stop  
* Download the SNIP program from the email that [use-snip.com](http://use-snip.com) sends, & install it.  
* In the Pushed-In Streams tab, press the “Add…” button to add a stream.   
* **Important:** when you first install SNIP you are given a small amount of time and starts where you have Basic authorization (which enables you to create mountpoint reservations and debug push-in server connections. You are advised to use this time wisely \- it can be hard to get connections running with just the right parameters.

![][image1]

* In the resulting “Add/Edit PUSH-In Reservation” pop-up dialog, entered the mount point name and a password for strsrv to use. N.B. Mountpoint name needs to be more than two characters long.

![][image2]

## Windows NTRIP Server Setup {#windows-ntrip-server-setup}

The NTRIP server pulls corrections out of your base station and sends them to a caster (or optionally, elsewhere). It is useful to test that you can push-in corrections to rtk2go.com from the Windows version of strsvr and can pull them from a rover and get an RTK\_FIXED fix.

Download RTKlib onto a Windows PC using instructions from [A Step-by-Step Guide for Downloading and Installing RTKLib](https://community.gpswebshop.com/2023/07/14/a-step-by-step-guide-to-downloading-and-installing-rtklib/)

* Launch strsrv and configure the serial input stream baud rate and com port, and set the output to be NTRIP Server

![][image3]

* Click the … under “Opt” and set the caster details and mount point and password that was previously configured in SNIP

![][image4]

* Pressed the Start button in STRSVR and SNIP starts showing data and logs related to the mountpoint data stream being provided by STRSVR

## Set up Linux NTRIP Server pushing to [rtk2go.com](http://rtk2go.com) {#set-up-linux-ntrip-server-pushing-to-rtk2go.com}

[Rtk2go.com](http://Rtk2go.com) uses SNIP (Simple NTRIP Server) to receive and distribute streams

* **Important:** if you are going to push to [rtk2go.com](http://rtk2go.com), be sure to register your mountpoint name first, and get a password, which you must use when the base station pushes its correction stream to [rtk2go.com](http://rtk2go.com)  
* Set up SNIP locally on a Windows machine and test your ability to push corrections to it from your linux base station NTRIP server. You learn a lot from the console output of SNIP, to help you get things debugged before you try to push to [rtk2go.com](http://rtk2go.com). Be aware of the limitations of the free SNIP license, and how the initial Basic license can be helpful  
* Set up a linux base station server. I used a Raspberry Pi Zero 2W and it worked fine.  
* Install rtklib:

sudo apt install rtklib

* Run the str2str command, which does the same thing as strsvr on Windows. Replace PASSWD with the password you received when you registered your mountpoint with [rtk2go.com](http://rtk2go.com). (If you are not using [rtk2go.com](http://rtk2go.com) your procedures may differ.)

str2str \-in serial://ttyUSB0:921600:8:n:1 \-out 'ntrips://paul.bouchier%40gmail.com:PASSWD@3.143.243.81:2101/LittleElm\_L1L5'

* The command line above pushes corrections to the LittleElm\_L1L5 mountpoint which was previously registered with [rtk2go.com](http://rtk2go.com). The command was figured out after much experimentation. Don’t trust what Gemini tells you. Note the use of %40 to encode the ‘@’ in your email address (username) given to you when you registered your endpoint with [rtk2go.com](http://rtk2go.com). The command line input section declares the baud rate to be 921600, and assumes you’ve changed it in the LC29H(BS) using the commands below (and installed a **rechargeable** battery so it gets remembered\!) The default baud rate is 115200 and would probably work fine but wasn’t tested. Note that str2str doesn’t know how to handle host names so [rtk2go.com](http://rtk2go.com) is replaced by its IP: 3.143.243.81. Declaring the stream type as ntrips (ntrip-server) is crucial.  
* The output of the str2str command should look like:

stream server start  
2026/06/11 07:18:19 \[CW---\]          0 B       0 bps (0) /dev/ttyUSB0 (1) connecting...   
2026/06/11 07:18:24 \[CC---\]       5240 B    8307 bps (0) /dev/ttyUSB0 (1) 3.143.243.81/LittleElm\_L1L5   
2026/06/11 07:18:29 \[CC---\]      10480 B    8304 bps (0) /dev/ttyUSB0 (1) 3.143.243.81/LittleElm\_L1L5 

* Check the [rtk2go.com](http://rtk2go.com) SNIP::STATUS page at [http://rtk2go.com:2101/SNIP::STATUS](http://rtk2go.com:2101/SNIP::STATUS) \- you will get a table of all known mountpoints. You should find your mountpoint there \- it will look like: 

| LittleElm\_L1L5  | Up | 21:24 MIN:SEC (1) 99.9% | RTCM 3.2 | 1.296 MB  \[\~8188bps\] |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |

### Create a service that starts the NTRIP server when the system boots {#create-a-service-that-starts-the-ntrip-server-when-the-system-boots}

* Open a terminal on your Raspberry Pi and run the following command to create and edit a new service file:

$ sudo nano /etc/systemd/system/str2str.service

* Paste the configuration block below into the editor

\[Unit\]  
Description=str2str RTCM Stream Service  
After=network-online.target  
Wants=network-online.target

\[Service\]  
ExecStart=/usr/local/bin/str2str \-in serial://ttyUSB0:921600:8:n:1 \-out 'ntrips://YOUR\_EMAIL%%40gmail.com:YOUR\_PASSWORD@3.143.243.81:2101/YOUR\_MOUNTPT'  
Restart=always  
RestartSec=5  
User=root

\[Install\]  
WantedBy=multi-user.target

* Change YOUR\_EMAIL, YOUR\_PASSWORD, YOUR\_MOUNTPT in the file to match what you registered with rtk2go.com  
* Note: The %40 in your email is escaped as %%40 because systemd treats single percent signs as special specifiers.  
* Verify the Executable Path. Systemd requires absolute file paths. Check where your str2str binary is installed by running:

$ which str2str

* If the output path is different than /usr/local/bin/str2str, change the path in the ExecStart= line above to match your output.  
* Enable and Start the Service. Save the file in nano by pressing Ctrl+O, Enter, and then exit using Ctrl+X.Run these three commands to load, start, and enable the service on boot:

$ sudo systemctl daemon-reload  
$ sudo systemctl enable str2str.service  
$ sudo systemctl start str2str.service

* Check the StatusVerify that your service is running smoothly without errors:

$ sudo systemctl status str2str.service

# Useful base station commands {#useful-base-station-commands}

### Restore Factory defaults PQTMRESTOREPAR {#restore-factory-defaults-pqtmrestorepar}

$PQTMRESTOREPAR\*\<Checksum\> restores all parameters set via $PQTM commands to default values

### Save parameters {#save-parameters}

$PQTMSAVEPAR\* saves the configurations set via $PQTM commands to NVM. Reset the module after running this command to get the parameters restored from NVM.

### Configure message rate PQTMCFGMSGRATE: {#configure-message-rate-pqtmcfgmsgrate:}

$PQTMCFGMSGRATE,W,\<MsgID\>,\<Rate\>\[,\<MsgVer\>\]\*\<Checksum\>\<CR\>\<LF\>   
W: Write mode (to change the setting).  
\<MsgID\>: The name of the message you want to configure (e.g., PQTMSVINSTATUS, GNGGA, GNRMC).  
\<Rate\>: Set to 0 to disable or 1 to enable (usually 1Hz).  
\<MsgVer\> (Optional): The protocol version for that specific message, often set to 1 or 2 depending on the message type.

### Survey in base station: PQTMCFGSVIN {#survey-in-base-station:-pqtmcfgsvin}

Sets/gets the survey-in feature. The base station receiver (i.e., the module) must work in static mode, which can be entered via Survey-in mode or Fixed mode. The Survey-in mode (\<Mode\> \= 1\) determines the receiver’s position by building a weighted mean of all valid 3D positioning solutions. You can set values of \<MinDur\> and \<3D\_AccLimit\> to define the minimum observation time and 3D position standard deviation used for the position estimation.  
The Fixed mode (\<Mode\> \= 2\) requires user to manually enter the receiver position coordinates. Any error in the base station position will translate directly into rover position error.  
//Set:  
$PQTMCFGSVIN,W,\<Mode\>,\<MinDur\>,\<3D\_AccLimit\>,\<ECEF\_X\>,\<ECEF\_Y\>,\<ECEF\_Z\>\*  
\<Mode\>Numeric-Configure the receiver mode.  
0 \= Disable  
1 \= Survey-in mode  
2 \= Fixed mode (ARP position is given in ECEF.)  
\<MinDur\>: number of valid samples required to complete survey-in. Range: 0–86400. Default value: 43200\.  
\<3D\_AccLimit\> 3D positioning accuracy **in decimeters** required for a sample to be accepted in survey-in mode. Default value: 15.0. If this field is 0, it means there is no limit.  
\<ECEF\_X/Y/Z\>: ECEF X, Y, Z coordinate when specifying position. Default value: 0.0.

//Get:  
$PQTMCFGSVIN,R\*  
Return the parameters retrieved from NVM last boot. Note: this is not the estimate of position made by running $PWTMCFGSVIN,W unless parameters were saved and the module rebooted.

## Other commands {#other-commands}

Connect to the unit using QGNSS. In the console, data input, Advanced, select: Text format: ASCII, Checksum Type: NMEA, Suffix: CRLF

In Data Input, enter the following (as per Quectel\_LC29HBS\_GNSS\_Protocol\_Specification\_V1.1-1.pdf) and from [https://github.com/Stefal/rtkbase/issues/425](https://github.com/Stefal/rtkbase/issues/425) 

Check firmware version:  
$PQTMVERNO\*  
Returns: $PQTMVERNO,LC29HBSNR11A01S,2023/02/13,10:14:06\*27  
The pertinent information to detect this is: LC29HBS

Enable MSM7 messages  
$PAIR432,1\*

Enable Station Reference Message 1005  
$PAIR434,1\*

Enable Ephemeris messages  
$PAIR436,1\*

Crank the baud rate up to 921600(Could also go 3000000\)  
$PAIR864,0,0,921600\*

Enable NMEA GGA Time, position, and fix related data. A good test of whether it’s working.  
$PAIR062,0,1\*

Enable NMEA GLL Position data: position fix, time of position fix, and status  
$PAIR062,1,1\*

Enable NMEA GSA GPS DOP and active satellites  
$PAIR062,2,1\*

Enable NMEA GSV Satellite information  
$PAIR062,3,1\*

Enable NMEA RMC Position, velocity, and time  
$PAIR062,4,1\*

Enable NMEA VTG Track made good and speed over ground  
$PAIR062,5,1\*

Enable NMEA ZDA UTC day, month, and year, and local time zone offset  
$PAIR062,6,1\*

Enable NMEA GRS GRS range residuals  
$PAIR062,7,1\*

Enable NMEA GST Position error statistics  
$PAIR062,8,1\*

Save parameters  
$PQTMSAVEPAR\*

Power cycle the unit, reconnect at the new baud rate. **Note**: when I entered these parameters it didn’t remember the setting to output  NMEA GGA etc across the reboot.

View \-\> binary. You should see messages like RTCM3: 1077, 1097\. These are the MSM7 high precision messages. **Issue:** the MSM7 messages didn’t come back for me after a board reset \- I had to re-issue the MSM7 command

* Enter this command: $PQTMCFGSVIN,W,1,3600,1,0,0,0  
* Add its checksum and send the command. The Text view should show the command being sent and acknowledged.  
* The command tells the device to survey in for an hour. Wait for an hour.

# Set up rover {#set-up-rover}

This section describes how to connect a rover LC29H(DA) to rtk2go and get an rtk fix. It assumes you have tmux installed.

* Clone the ntrip\_client repo into ros2\_ws/src, check out the bugfix/rtk2go branch and build it. Note: this fork of ntrip\_client works with [rtk2go.com](http://rtk2go.com) \- as of this writing the upstream repo doesn’t. Also clone an LC29H(DA) driver

$ cd ros2\_ws  
$ git clone git@github.com:PaulBouchier/ntrip\_client.git src/ntrip\_client  
$ git clone [git@github.com](mailto:git@github.com):amaltp13/GPS-RTK-Localisation.git src/GPS-RTK-Localization  
$ cd src/ntrip\_client  
$ git checkout bugfix/ntrip\_client  
$cd ../..  
$ colcon build –symlink-install  
$ source install/setup.bash

* Start the ntrip\_client

$ ros2 launch ntrip\_client ntrip\_client\_launch.py host:=rtk2go.com port:=2101 mountpoint:=LittleElm\_L1L5 username:=YOUR\_EMAIL password:=none authenticate:=true

* Note: the ntrip\_client often fails the first time it attempts to connect, but succeeds on a subsequent attempt. It should print something like the following:

\[ntrip\_ros.py-1\] \[INFO\] \[1781169852.867859836\] \[ntrip\_client\]: Connected to http://rtk2go.com:2101/LittleElm\_L1L5

* In a new window, start an LC29(DA) client

$ cd src/GPS-RTK-Localization  
$ bash ./run\_rtk\_tmux.sh

# References {#references}

* Waveshare wiki: [https://www.waveshare.com/wiki/LC29H(XX)\_GPS/RTK\_HAT?srsltid=AfmBOooN5FIkS2fNU3xfwcKuo2eZ5A7IUS9NhGsLtwSIC0H8m00jqYhd\#Software](https://www.waveshare.com/wiki/LC29H\(XX\)_GPS/RTK_HAT?srsltid=AfmBOooN5FIkS2fNU3xfwcKuo2eZ5A7IUS9NhGsLtwSIC0H8m00jqYhd#Software)   
* Issue 425 in the rtkbase repo: Lots of discussion by folks trying to get LC29H(BS) configured and working with rtkbase [https://github.com/Stefal/rtkbase/issues/425](https://github.com/Stefal/rtkbase/issues/425)   
* Karim’s notes at [https://docs.google.com/document/d/1ThXE5ZSWL7XSKh6SvdmiFhDySky-uoi28JDZUchsnYw/edit?pli=1\&tab=t.0\#heading=h.nc339kga2h82](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1ThXE5ZSWL7XSKh6SvdmiFhDySky-uoi28JDZUchsnYw/edit?pli=1&tab=t.0#heading=h.nc339kga2h82)  
* Rtkbase is a Linux helper tool for surveying in an LC29H base station. It also contains strsvr \- the stream server software that pulls corrections out of the LC29H (BS), and can also push to rtk2go.  [https://github.com/deece/rtkbase/tree/lc29hbs](https://github.com/deece/rtkbase/tree/lc29hbs)  
* Step-by-step how to set up strsvr on Windows: [https://community.gpswebshop.com/2023/11/26/setting-up-a-free-ntrip-server-with-rtklibs-strsvr/](https://community.gpswebshop.com/2023/11/26/setting-up-a-free-ntrip-server-with-rtklibs-strsvr/) 

# Survey In the base station (Approach \#2, untested) {#survey-in-the-base-station-(approach-#2,-untested)}

AI suggested an alternate approach to surveying in the base station. It is recorded here as a possibly interesting alternative.

#### **Forget the Passive Dirt Monuments: Use the NOAA CORS Network** {#forget-the-passive-dirt-monuments:-use-the-noaa-cors-network}

If you are setting up an RTK base station on a fixed structure (like a house, shop, or tripod on your property) and need its absolute coordinate, hunting down a physical brass disk in the weeds isn't always the most accurate route anymore.

Instead, you can record a raw GNSS log (usually an **RINEX** file) on your base station for a few hours, then upload it to **OPUS** (Online Positioning User Service), which is managed by NOAA.

OPUS will use the **CORS** (Continuously Operating Reference Stations) network—permanent, high-grade government GNSS base stations operating across Texas—to triangulate your base station's exact position relative to the global framework down to the millimeter. This eliminates the need to physically find a brass disk or worry if a tractor hit it ten years ago.

# Testing Results {#testing-results}

## 6/14/26 {#6/14/26}

**Testing consistency of fix and correspondence to RTK\_FIXED drops**  
Morning test with LC29H base & rover antennas side by side on roof outside my office, partially shaded by huge tree. Duration \~4 hours. I had done a 6-hour survey-in starting the preceding night, and extending into a thunderstorm, so the survey-in may be inaccurate. A bagfile was recorded \- Ref: rosbag2\_2026\_06\_14-09\_45\_10  
![][image5]  
![][image6]  
For the first almost 2 hours the rover reported it was in RTK\_FIXED (with a couple of dropouts to RTK\_FLOAT, yet it clearly wasn’t in RTK\_FIXED \- X drifted 0.4m, and y drifted \> 1m. Also it reported the rover was \~4m from the base but it was only 10cm away. After 2 hours it seemed to lock in and become stable, but maintained a Y offset of about 2m from actual rover position relative to base antenna. This was traced to an error in the local origin coordinates. When fixed, it did report it was offset 10cm. **Conclusion:** when the fix became stable, it was correct, but RTK\_FIXED status was reported while it wasn’t actually locked in for a couple of hours.

**Opinion:** It was recommended to me that in order for the base station to work well, it needs: 1\. really good sky and 2\. a really high accuracy location and 3\. a very good antenna. 

## 6/15/26

A ZED-F9P antenna was placed on the other side of the base station antenna from the LC29H rover, so was about 20cm away, on the same rooftop as show above. Both the LC29H rover and the ZED-F9P were run side-by-side on the same computer. Initially I tried providing corrections from 3 mountpoints to both the LC29H rover and the ZED-F9P, and the results were somewhat inconsistent but broadly looked like:

* Corrections from LittleElm\_L1L5 (corrections for L1 & L5, no corrections for L2). As expected, the LC29H rover reported it was 10cm from base. Surprisingly, the ZED-F9P reported a position only 15cm from the base. Possibly it found RTK\_FIXED from L1 corrections alone?  
* Corrections from EMBER1 (corrections for L1 and L2, no corrections for L5). As expected, LC29H reported 2m different fix than with LittleElm\_L1L5 but surprisingly it reported it had RTK\_FIXED from EMBER1. It’s surprising because there were no L5 corrections. This was a short test and the ZED-F9P never really got dialed in, no results reported.  
* Corrections from VN1 (believed to provide corrections for L1, L2 and L5). A bagfile was gathered for almost 2 hours and the LC29H and ZED-F9P lat/lon fixes are plotted together below. Note: the last decimal place of the fix represents about 11cm. Ref: rosbag2\_2026\_06\_15-08\_43\_09

![][image7]  
**Conclusions:**

* The ZED-F9P took about 45 minutes to get stable. It jumped a cycle and got much closer to the LC29H (average 10-20cm) which is in the ballpark of reality, but there was sizable drift. Maybe the big tree is an issue?  
* The LC29H experienced two RTK\_FIXED dropouts but the fix didn’t really change  
* Both the LC29H and ZED-F9P fix wandered about 20cm. Maybe the big tree is an issue?  
* The LC29H seems to wander in and out of RTK\_FIXED and RTK\_FLOAT whether it has corrections or not \- that flag can’t be relied upon. This is a big concern, if you can’t tell whether you can rely on the fix. OTOH it matches my experience with PPP GPS \- high precision GPS receivers (even from reputable companies like Novatel) will lie and say they have a high precision fix, but simultaneously jump a half meter.  
* I need to test with wide open sky

[image2]: <data:image/png;base64,>

[image3]: <data:image/png;base64,>

[image4]: <data:image/png;base64,>

[image5]: <data:image/png;base64,>

[image6]: <data:image/png;base64,>

[image7]: <data:image/png;base64,>
